hits.title: Preparatory Algebra
hits.description: Covers the necessary mathematical tools needed to succeed in our algebra course and provides fundamental mathematical skills. Topics include real numbers, linear equations and inequalities, integral and fractional exponents, polynomials and their arithmetic, polynomial equations and equations with fractional exponents, the quadratic formula and completing the square, systems of two linear equations, graphing, and problem solving using algebra and graphs. All students must pass an assessment on basic mathematical skills to complete the course. The course does not count for degree credit.

footnotes:
The WSB has standardized the waitlist process.  Students looking to access a closed class should put their name on the waitlist and waitlists will be processed on a first-come first-served basis.  The only exception is GEN BUS 306, for which business students will be given priority.  If you need assistance adding yourself to the waitlist or if you wish to request an exception to the first-come, first-served process, please use this link to request permission: 
         https://go.wisc.edu/busenroll 

Please note that any requests for an exception to the first-come, first-served waitlist processing will be verified through advising.  

All undergraduate Business courses are restricted to admitted Business students and Certificate in Business students declared prior to Spring 2025 (does not include Certificates in Entrepreneurship, Consulting, Accounting Fundamentals, Risk Management and Insurance, or Summer Certificate in Business Fundamentals) until 8 AM (CST) May 1, 2025 for fall enrollment and 8 AM (CST) December 9, 2025 for spring enrollment, unless otherwise noted. ACCT I S 300 is not restricted.

Certificate in Business students declared after Spring 2025 will gain access to ACCT I S 100, M H R 300, FINANCE 300, MARKETNG 300, OTM 300, REAL EST 306, and R M I 300 at least 3 days prior to the noted open enrollment date. Spring 2026 enrollment date for CIB is December 4, 2025.

hits.title: Introductory Financial Accounting
hits.description: Examines generally accepted accounting principles for measurement and reporting of financial information in a balance sheet, income statement, and statement of cash flows; introduction to analysis and interpretation of financial accounting data for decision-making purposes.

footnotes: Questions? Please contact Andrew Bartsch, Advisor:  apbartsch@wisc.edu or (608) 262-2041

hits.title: Introduction to African Cultural Expression
hits.description: An introduction to current research in African cultural studies, ranging from various literary genres and uses of discourse; to diverse media including screen media, music, cartoons, and journalism; and to other forms of popular expression like soccer and drama. Through an introduction to these forms, study the diverse methods used by scholars in this field, such as textual analysis, film criticism, ethnomusicology, discourse analysis, and ethnography; and to the cultural diversity of Africa and its diasporic cultures.

footnotes: Denise Wiyaka, Director, 317 Ingraham Hall, (608)263-5501

hits.title: Introduction to American Indian Studies
hits.description: Focus is history, social organization, political experience, artistic expression of North American Indians, using methods and materials from a number of disciplines as an introduction to the interdisciplinary field.

hits.enrollmentPrerequisites: Not open to students declared in Art BS, Art BFA, or Art Education BS
hits.title: Introduction to Art
hits.description: Seek answers to the fundamental question "What is art?" from multiple perspectives such as historical, theoretical, critical, conceptual, formal, and experiential. Develop visual literacy, sophisticated observational skills and a formal language to assist in the interpretation of objects and experiences in the context of art. Through both theory and practice, develop an understanding of the ways artists arrive at the ideas that inform their creative processes. Includes a survey of developments in art media and looks broadly at art movements, trends and styles throughout history and in varied world cultures.

footnotes:
For more information about courses, majors, and certificates offered by the Department of Asian Languages and Cultures, see alc.wisc.edu

For concerns that cannot be answered by the course instructor, please contact the department's Student Services Coordinator: Rachel Weiss, rweiss@wisc.edu.

topics.longDescription: Exploring Asian History and Culture Through Sports

hits.title: Exploring Asia Through Sports
hits.description: Offers a comparative and interdisciplinary introduction to multiple cultures of Asia. Possible topics include but are not limited to: travelogues; the languages of Asia; food cultures of Asia.

instructorProvidedContent.instructorDescription:
In an often-divided world, sport serves as a unique connective tissue that brings people together across and within societies. At the same time, it can also deepen divisions through unequal access, national rivalries, and the commercialization of sport.

This course examines both Western sports introduced to Asia and Asian sports that have achieved global recognition, including martial arts and e-sports. Using sport as a lens, students will explore topics such as colonial history, racial and cultural identities, imagined communities, migration, gender, disability, and the political and economic dimensions of athletic competition.

Assigned readings will be complemented by documentary and feature films that illustrate the social and historical impact of various sports in Asia. Guest speakers specializing in different sports and regions will enrich our exploration. Class activities include in-person and online discussions, media reviews, and documentation of experiential learning. No midterm or final exam

By the end of the course, students will be able to critically analyze how sports reflect and shape cultural identities, power relations, and global connections across Asia and beyond.

footnotes:
For more information about courses, majors, and certificates offered by the Department of Asian Languages and Cultures, see alc.wisc.edu

For concerns that cannot be answered by the course instructor, please contact the department's Student Services Coordinator: Rachel Weiss, rweiss@wisc.edu.

hits.enrollmentPrerequisites: Students required to take the MSN ESLAT cannot enroll until the ESL 118 requirement is satisfied. Not open to students who have credit for COM ARTS 105 or 181.
hits.title: Introduction to Speech Composition
hits.description: Develop written and spoken communication skills through hands-on learning while studying audience- centered public speaking. Focus on becoming more confident and comfortable as a speaker and writer. Strengthen your library research skills and digital literacy. Gain essential skills for success in college courses and life beyond the university, from job interviews to professional presentations, to research talks.

instructorProvidedContent.instructorDescription:
Why Choose Com Arts 100?

Effective communication skills are vital for every course, job, and life endeavor.

Enroll in Com Arts 100 to:
 	o Master the art of clearly and effectively communicating your ideas to different audiences.
 	o Sharpen your critical and creative thinking.
 	o Build your confidence as a speaker.
 	o Enhance your ability to inform, persuade, and commemorate others.

The skills you develop in Com Arts 100 will assist you in future courses and professional settings, such as networking and job interviews.

What do students say about Com Arts 100?

"Of all my classes, I think my speech course is the most interesting and useful. . . . Besides improving my speaking skills, I've also learned to write and listen better. . . . The thing I like most about this class is the personal attention each person received due to its small size."

"It's a great confidence booster. "

"Very worthwhile class that helped me grow as a speaker and as a writer -- something I didn't expect -- and it was quite fun."

"I learned far more than just speech communication; I learned about myself and the world."

footnotes:
Department Chair: Christa Olson, 7187 Helen C. White Hall

hits.title: Historical Studies: Introductory
hits.description: Introduction to elementary-level historical studies at the research university. Emphasis on interpretation and critical thinking. Topics vary.

footnotes: Curricular Rep, Dept of Kinesiology, (608)262-0259 or ugkines@education.wisc.edu

hits.title: Exercise, Nutrition, and Health
hits.description: Guidelines and assessment methods for fitness and nutrition. Motivation, adherence and stress-reduction techniques discussed. Lecture-demonstration concerning effects of exercise and nutrition on health and well-being.

footnotes:
The following LSC courses give priority access to LSC majors from November 11 to November 13 - LSC 212, LSC 314, LSC 332, LSC 450, LSC 460, LSC 532, LSC 625 and LSC 640.

The following LSC courses give priority access LSC majors, Science Communication and Digital Studies certificate students from November 14 to November 17 - LSC 212, LSC 314, LSC 332, LSC 450, LSC 460, LSC 532, LSC 625 and LSC 640.

LSC courses will open to ALL students on November 18.

You must be declared in the LSC major, Sci Comm certificate or Digital Studies certificate programs to receive priority enrollment. If you see a class that says it is only open to LSC majors, Sci Comm certificate students, or Digital Studies certificate students, please check back later to enroll in any open seats or add yourself to the course waitlist.

Most of our courses provide additional information under "Instructor Provided Content" at the bottom of this tab. Check out that information to learn more about the course.

Registration questions can be directed to lifescicomm@cals.wisc.edu.

hits.enrollmentPrerequisites: Students required to take the MSN ESLAT cannot enroll until the ESL 118 requirement is satisfied
hits.title: Science and Storytelling
hits.description: Fundamentals of effective written and spoken communication. Develops skills in gathering and evaluating information, writing research papers and other documents, and preparing and delivering oral presentations.

hits.enrollmentPrerequisites: Not open to students with credit for MICROBIO 101 or 303.
hits.title: The Microbial World
hits.description: Primarily for non-science majors.  Roles of microorganisms and viruses in nature, health, agriculture, pollution control and ecology. Principles of disease production, epidemiology and body defense mechanisms. Biotechnology and the genetic engineering revolution.

footnotes: Contact Lisa Cappabianca at ugkines@education.wisc.edu if you have questions about enrollment.

hits.title: Orientation to Occupational Therapy
hits.description: An opportunity to explore occupational therapy as a potential career; introduction to the general concepts of human occupation and adaptation; survey of OT practice settings and clinical groups.

footnotes:
Undergraduate students: Contact Rebecca Bradbury, rebecca.bradbury@wisc.edu, for questions regarding requisites and course permissions.

Graduate students: Contact Kayla Armstrong, armstrongalf@wisc.edu, for questions regarding requisites and course permissions.

hits.title: Disability and Society
hits.description: Provides introductory and interdisciplinary life-span perspectives on disability, relevant for both education and non-education majors. Introduces theoretical, cultural, and political models of disability and explores the lived experiences of persons with disabilities (or people who are perceived to have disabilities) in society.

footnotes: Enrollment questions? Contact enrollment@socwork.wisc.edu

hits.enrollmentPrerequisites: First year students or first year transfer students only
hits.title: So You Want to Change the World?
hits.description: Examine ideas and values related to making a difference, think critically about the meaning and methods of changing the world, and integrate thoughtful evidence with your values and beliefs about doing good in the world. With a focus on the profession of social work and other helping professions, consider a variety of social problems, and investigate and critique various approaches to creating change to improve social problems.

input: So You Want to Change the World?

hits.title: Experiencing Theatre
hits.description: By attending live theatre performances, watching films of theatrical productions and participating in colloquiums with theatre professionals, explore how theatre is made through the lens of various theatre artists. Through this exploration, develop an understanding and a deeper  appreciation of performative events as well as critically think about those experiences. Explores performance and the human condition, using acquired knowledge to build empathy and appreciation for the complexities of one's own and other people's perspectives.

footnotes:
Christy Clark-Pujara Chair, 4143 H.C. White Hall,
Please email aas.registration@hcw.wisc.edu for any registration questions

hits.title: Introduction to African American Studies
hits.description: Delve into the origins of African American Studies as an academic discipline and explore its early forms. Examine how African Americans have contributed to the understanding of the African American experience, both historically and in the present day. Analyze current struggles of the Black community for freedom, justice, and humanity.
